attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with worsening shortness of breath. She now finds it difficult to complete household chores and has dyspnoea at rest. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, ischaemic cardiomyopathy (anterior wall myocardial infarction 5 years ago) and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (ejection fraction 30% 1 year ago). Her medications include losartan, carvedilol, aspirin, atorvastatin, metformin, and empagliflozin.

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 62/min, blood pressure 160/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 19/min, SpO2 93% on room air, and temperature 36.5 °C. She has bibasal crackles, an S3 gallop, and bilateral pitting oedema to her knees.

An ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy with Q waves and T-wave inversions in V1 to V5. A chest X-ray reveals cardiomegaly and pulmonary vascular congestion. Laboratory values, including complete blood count, creatinine and potassium, are within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following has the greatest evidence for long term mortality benefit in this patient?
attributes.explanation:
The patient has heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), and is currently presenting in a decompensated congestive state, with evidence of pulmonary oedema. Her symptoms mean she is currently NYHA class IV.

Spironolactone is a potassium-sparing diuretic which works as a mineralocorticoid or aldosterone receptor antagonist (MRA). In patients with HFrEF, diuretics can be used to improve symptoms of congestion. Adding spironolactone is recommended in patients with HFrEF with ongoing symptoms despite regular ACE-I or ARB therapy as it has strong evidence for long term mortality benefit.

Isosorbide dinitrate is an antianginal medication. It is a vasodilator and works by dilating the blood vessels, which reduces afterload and improves coronary blood flow. This patient has no symptoms of angina but nitrate therapy may be considered where there are refractory symptoms despite trials of ACE-I, ARB, and ARNI (angiotensin receptor/neprilsyn inhibitor).

Hydralazine is a vasodilator that is used to treat high blood pressure and heart failure. It works by dilating the blood vessels, which reduces afterload. It may be considered as an additional treatment option where ACE, ARB and ARNI are contraindicated.

Digoxin is used to treat heart failure and atrial fibrillation. It works by increasing the force of the heart's contractions and slowing the heart rate. Digoxin may be considered in combination with nitrate therapy for refractory cases of HFrEF.

Ivabradine is a medication that selectively inhibits the funny current (If) in the sinus node of the heart, resulting in a lower heart rate. It can be considered for HFrEF patients with heart rate of equal or more than 70/min and LVEF less than or equal to 35%. By reducing heart rate, ivabradine helps to decrease myocardial oxygen demand and improve symptoms in patients with heart failure.

Management of congested HFrEF includes ACE-I or ARB therapy, an aldosterone receptor antagonist such as spironolactone, and the addition of a heart failure B-blocker once euvolemic. SGLT2 inhibitors (like empagliflozin that the patient is already taking) have also shown significant mortality benefit in HFrEF and are now considered part of the four pillars of HFrEF therapy alongside ACE-I/ARB, beta-blockers, and MRAs. These medications should be titrated to maximally tolerated doses. Implantable cardioverter defibrillators (ICD) are another important consideration for selected HFrEF patients with broad QRS complexes, LVEF of 35% and refractory symptoms despite maximal medical therapy.
learningPoints: All patients with heart failure and reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) should be on maximally tolerated doses of ACE-I or ARB, beta-blocker, MRA such as spironolactone, and SGLT2 inhibitor.
Spironolactone has strong evidence for long-term mortality benefit in HFrEF patients with ongoing symptoms despite ACE-I or ARB therapy.
ICD and Ivabradine can be considered in selected HFrEF patients with refractory symptoms and LVEF of 35% or less.
references: Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/

National Heart Foundation of Australia and Cardiac Society of Australia and New Zealand (Chung, M., Liew, D., et al.). (2018). Guidelines for the prevention, detection and management of heart failure in Australia. Melbourne: National Heart Foundation of Australia.

Atherton JJ, Audehm R, Connell C. Heart failure guidelines A concise summary for the GP. Medicine Today. 2019; 20:14-24. Available from: https://medicinetoday.com.au/mt/2019/june/feature-article/heart-failure-guidelines-concise-summary-gp

Heidenreich PA, Bozkurt B, Aguilar D, Allen LA, Byun JJ, Colvin MM, Deswal A, Drazner MH, Dunlay SM, Evers LR, Fang JC, Fedson SE, Fonarow GC, Hayek SS, Hernandez AF, Khazanie P, Kittleson MM, Lee CS, Link MS, Milano CA, Nnacheta LC, Sandhu AT, Stevenson LW, Vardeny O, Vest AR, Yancy CW; ACC/AHA Joint Committee Members. 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure: A Report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Joint Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines.
